The Hidden Yogic Meaning Behind the Praises of Tārā in Twenty-one Verses #1

# The Hidden Yogic Meaning Behind the Praises of Tārā in Twenty-one Verses \#1

### By Tārānātha

[](https://substack.com/@tseringdorji)

#### Sanskrit verse

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RoCN!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe243ee93-047f-405b-9c1f-e7a5457a3bc4_1277x250.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RoCN!,w_2400,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe243ee93-047f-405b-9c1f-e7a5457a3bc4_1277x250.png)

namaḥ śakrānalabrahmamarudviśveśvarārcite\|  
  
bhūtavetālagandharvagaṇayakṣapuraskṛte \|\|

#### Tibetan verse

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Text within this block will maintain its original spacing when published

``` text །ཕྱག་འཚལ་བརྒྱ་བྱིན་མེ་ལྷ་ཚངས་པ། 
།རླུང་ལྷ་སྣ་ཚོགས་དབང་ཕྱུག་མཆོད་མ། 
།འབྱུང་པོ་རོ་ལངས་དྲི་ཟ་རྣམས་དང་། 
།གནོད་སྦྱིན་ཚོགས་ཀྱིས་མདུན་ནས་བསྟོད་མ།། ```

#### English translation \[of Tibetan text\]

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Homage to you \[Tārā\], praised by Indra, Agni, Brahmā, Maruts, and Śiva. All the hosts of bhūtas, vetālas, Gandharvas and yakṣas pay tribute to you.

#### Hidden meaning behind this verse \[by Tārānātha\]

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

namaḥ śakra anala brahma marud viśva-īśvara arcite\|

bhūta vetāla gandharva gaṇa yakṣa puraskṛte \|\|

The five gods represent the five elements: *Śakra* \[Indra\] as the earth, Anala as the fire, Maruta as the air and Viśva-īśvara \[Shiva\] as the sky. These five gods worshipping \[arcita\] at the feet of Tārā means that the five elements dissolve into the bindu.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r8yw!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F329d2834-3665-4d4b-8995-133f1248be61_519x604.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r8yw!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F329d2834-3665-4d4b-8995-133f1248be61_519x604.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The five spirits represent the five bodily elements: Bhūta \[ghost\] as channels, Vetāla \[walking dead\] as bindu, Gandharva \[scent eater\] as vital air, and the Yaksha \[bringer of harm\] as conceptual thought. These five spirits honouring Tara means that these five bodily elements also dissolve into the bindu.

**Note**:

- I found the Sanskrit text in the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon -
  Books (dsbcproject.org) tārānamaskāraikaviṃśatistotram

- English translation of Tibetan verse is from Lotsawa website:
  Translated by [Samye
  Translations](https://www.lotsawahouse.org/translators/samye-translations/)
  (trans. [Stefan
  Mang](https://www.lotsawahouse.org/translators/stefan-mang/), [Peter
  Woods](https://www.lotsawahouse.org/translators/peter-woods/), and
  [Ryan Conlon](https://www.lotsawahouse.org/translators/ryan-conlon/),
  ed. Libby Hogg) with the kind assistance of [Adam
  Pearcey](https://www.lotsawahouse.org/translators/adam-pearcey/),
  2019.

- Taranatha's explanation is taken from his text ‘Commentary on the
  praises to Tārā.
